open-cover.definition: A collection of open sets whose union contains $K$. A finite subcover is a finite subcollection that still covers $K$.

compact.definition: A set $K$ such that every open cover of $K$ has a finite subcover.

heine-borel.definition: A subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

blocks.content: Last lesson a **closed** set was one containing all its limit points. Closedness traps boundary points; today we add a second property that tames *infinity* itself.

Here is the hook. Cover the open interval $(0,1)$ with the open sets $(1/n, 1)$ for $n = 2, 3, 4, \dots$ Their union is all of $(0,1)$ — every point eventually gets in. But pick *finitely many* of them: there is a largest denominator $N$, the union is just $(1/N, 1)$, and everything in $(0, 1/N]$ is left out in the cold.

On $(0,1)$ you can never trim the cover down to a finite list. On the closed interval $[0,1]$ you always can. That single difference — *can finitely many always replace infinitely many?* — is **compactness**, and it is exactly what makes theorems like the Extreme Value Theorem work.

Let us state it precisely. An **open cover** of a set $K$ is a collection $\{G_\alpha\}$ of open sets whose union contains $K$. A **finite subcover** is a finite subcollection $G_{\alpha_1}, \dots, G_{\alpha_k}$ that *still* covers $K$.

blocks.latex: K \subseteq \bigcup_\alpha G_\alpha \quad\text{(open cover)} \qquad K \subseteq G_{\alpha_1} \cup \cdots \cup G_{\alpha_k} \quad\text{(finite subcover)}

blocks.content: **Definition.** $K$ is **compact** if *every* open cover of $K$ has a finite subcover. The word *every* is the whole game: to prove a set is compact you must handle all covers at once, but to prove it is **not** compact you need just **one** bad cover with no finite subcover.

steps.prompt: Slide $n$ upward and watch the open set $(1/n, 1)$ stretch leftward toward $0$. Each individual set is captured by the union, yet $0$'s neighborhood is reached only in the *limit* — no single set, and no finite batch, ever covers everything down to $0$. This is the cover $\{(1/n,1)\}$ that defeats $(0,1)$.
Worked example: prove that $(0,1)$ is **not** compact.

blocks.content: **Strategy.** Exhibit one open cover with no finite subcover. We use $\mathcal{G} = \{\, (1/n, 1) : n \ge 2 \,\}$.
**It is a cover.** If $x \in (0,1)$ then $x > 0$, so the Archimedean property gives an $n$ with $1/n < x$; hence $x \in (1/n, 1)$. Thus $(0,1) \subseteq \bigcup_{n\ge 2}(1/n,1)$.
**No finite subcover.** Take any finite subcollection $(1/n_1,1), \dots, (1/n_k,1)$ and let $N = \max_i n_i$. Since these intervals are nested, their union is the single largest one:
**Conclusion.** The point $1/N$ (and everything in $(0, 1/N]$) lies in $(0,1)$ but is missed. So no finite subcollection covers $(0,1)$, and $(0,1)$ is not compact. $\blacksquare$

steps.prompt: Same cover $\{(1/n,1) : n \ge 2\}$, but now of the half-open interval $(0, 1]$. You pick the finite subcollection with largest denominator $N$; its union is $(1/N, 1]$ restricted to the set — i.e. it covers everything except a leftover near $0$. Which set of points is left uncovered?

workedExample.content: Mirror the worked example. A finite subcollection has a largest denominator $N$, and the union of nested intervals is the single largest interval $(1/N, 1)$.
Now ask which points of the target set are *not* inside $(1/N,1)$ — they are the small positive numbers at or below $1/N$.

choices.label: The points in $(0,\, 1/N]$ — a sliver near $0$.
None; the finite subcollection already covers everything.

feedback.correct: Right. The finite union is $(1/N,1)$, so every $x$ with $0 < x \le 1/N$ escapes. A leftover near $0$ survives every finite choice, so $(0,1]$ is not compact either.

incorrect.message: A finite subcollection has a *largest* denominator $N$ and unions to $(1/N,1)$. That cannot reach below $1/N$, so points must be left over.
The trouble is always at the open end near $0$, not at $1$. Find the largest $N$ used and ask what sits at or below $1/N$.
Use the no-finite-subcover move: largest denominator $N$ gives union $(1/N,1)$, leaving the sliver $(0,1/N]$ uncovered.

blocks.content: Which sets *are* compact? Two facts hold in **any** metric space:

**(1) Compact $\Rightarrow$ bounded.** Cover $K$ by the balls $B(p, n)$ of radius $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ around a fixed point $p$. These cover everything, so compactness yields a finite subcover; the largest ball $B(p, N)$ then contains all of $K$. A set that is not bounded (like $[0,\infty)$ or $\mathbb{R}$) can never be compact.

**(2) Compact $\Rightarrow$ closed.** A point outside a compact set can be separated from it by a cover of small balls, so the complement is open. Together: every compact set is **closed and bounded**.

blocks.latex: K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \text{ is compact} \iff K \text{ is closed and bounded.}

blocks.content: Two more tools: a **closed subset of a compact set is compact**, and (a preview of the next unit) the **continuous image of a compact set is compact** — the engine behind the Extreme Value Theorem.

steps.prompt: Working in $\mathbb{R}$, which of these sets is **compact**?

feedback.correct: Yes. $[0,1]$ is closed and bounded, so Heine–Borel makes it compact.

incorrect.message: $(0,1)$ is bounded but **not closed** (it misses its limit points $0$ and $1$). The cover $\{(1/n,1)\}$ has no finite subcover.
$[0,\infty)$ is closed but **not bounded**, so it fails Heine–Borel. Unbounded sets are never compact.
$\mathbb{R}$ is closed but unbounded. Compactness requires closed **and** bounded.
Apply Heine–Borel: the compact set must be both closed and bounded. Only one option is both.

steps.prompt: Here is a *flawed* proof that the closed set $[0,\infty)$ is compact. Exactly one step is invalid — select it.

steps.label: $[0,\infty)$ is closed: it contains all of its limit points.
By Heine–Borel, every closed subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact.

feedback.correct: Right — step 2 drops a hypothesis. Heine–Borel requires closed **and bounded**, not closed alone. Indeed the cover $\{(-1, n) : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ of $[0,\infty)$ has no finite subcover, so $[0,\infty)$ is not compact.

incorrect.message: Step 1 is correct: $[0,\infty)$ really is closed. The error is in how Heine–Borel is then applied.
Step 3 follows logically from step 2; the rot is in step 2 itself. Recall the *full* hypothesis of Heine–Borel.
Heine–Borel is an 'iff' with **two** conditions — closed AND bounded. Find the step that uses only half of them.

steps.prompt: Reconstruct the proof that **every compact set $K$ is bounded**. Match each step to the reason that justifies it.

steps.label: The balls $B(p,n)$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, form an open cover of $K$.
There is a finite subcover $B(p,n_1), \dots, B(p,n_k)$.
Hence $K \subseteq B(p,N)$ where $N = \max_i n_i$, so $K$ is bounded.

justifications.label: Each ball is open and every point lies within some finite distance of $p$, so their union contains $K$.
Compactness: every open cover of $K$ has a finite subcover.
Finitely many nested balls have a largest one that contains all the rest, giving a single bounding radius.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Cover $K$ by growing balls, extract a finite subcover by compactness, and the largest ball bounds $K$. Compactness converts an infinite cover into one radius.

incorrect.message: Trace the canonical move: building the cover (the balls are open and exhaust the space), invoking compactness (finite subcover), then collapsing finitely many nested balls into the largest one.

blocks.content: **Compactness** is the finite-subcover property: $K$ is compact when every open cover of $K$ admits a finite subcover. To *disprove* compactness, exhibit one cover with no finite subcover — as $\{(1/n,1)\}$ does for $(0,1)$.

In any metric space, compact $\Rightarrow$ closed and bounded. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ the converse also holds — that is **Heine–Borel**: compact $\iff$ closed and bounded. So $[0,1]$ and $\{0\}\cup\{1/n\}$ are compact, while $(0,1)$ (not closed) and $[0,\infty)$ (not bounded) are not.

blocks.latex: K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \text{ compact} \iff K \text{ closed and bounded}

blocks.content: Why it matters: compactness lets *finitely many* stand in for *infinitely many*. That is precisely what powers the Extreme Value Theorem, uniform continuity on $[a,b]$, and the fact that continuous images of compact sets are compact — the arc the next unit picks up.

items.prompt: The Heine–Borel theorem states that a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if it is:

feedback.correct: Exactly — in $\mathbb{R}^n$, compact means closed and bounded, no more and no less.

incorrect.message: Compact sets are closed, not open: a non-empty open set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is never compact. The two conditions are closed and *bounded*.
Boundedness, not infinitude, is the second condition. $\{0\}$ is finite and compact; $\mathbb{N}$ is infinite, closed, yet not compact.
Recall the exact pair of hypotheses in Heine–Borel: closed AND bounded.

items.prompt: Which set is **closed but not compact** (in $\mathbb{R}$)?

feedback.correct: Right. $[0,\infty)$ is closed but unbounded, so Heine–Borel fails — unboundedness blocks compactness.

incorrect.message: $[0,1]$ is closed *and* bounded, hence compact. We want closed but **not** compact, i.e. closed and unbounded.
$(0,1)$ is not closed at all (it misses $0$ and $1$). We need a set that is closed yet fails to be compact — look for an unbounded one.
Compact = closed and bounded. 'Closed but not compact' means closed and **unbounded**.

items.prompt: Self-explanation: arrange the steps proving $(0,1)$ is **not** compact via the cover $\{(1/n,1)\}$, from first to last.

items.label: Let $N$ be the largest denominator appearing in it.
Since the intervals are nested, their union is just $(1/N, 1)$.
The sliver $(0, 1/N]$ is left uncovered, so no finite subcover exists.

feedback.correct: Exactly the logic. The reason this works: a *finite* list always has a largest denominator $N$, and a finite union of nested intervals can only reach down to $1/N$ — never all the way to $0$.

incorrect.message: Order the canonical no-finite-subcover move: start with an arbitrary finite subcollection, extract its largest denominator $N$, collapse the nested union to $(1/N,1)$, then point to the uncovered sliver $(0,1/N]$.

retrievalBank.prompt: An **open cover** of a set $K$ is best described as:

choices.label: a collection of open sets whose union contains $K$
a single open set equal to $K$
a finite list of points dense in $K$

feedback.correct: Yes — any collection of open sets whose union swallows $K$. Compactness then asks whether finitely many of them already suffice.

incorrect.message: A cover may use many open sets, and it need not equal $K$ — it only has to *contain* $K$ in its union.
A cover is made of open *sets*, not points. The defining property is that their union contains $K$.
Open cover = a family of open sets whose union contains $K$.

retrievalBank.prompt: Which set is **bounded but not compact** (in $\mathbb{R}$)?

feedback.correct: Right. $(0,1)$ is bounded but not closed, and the cover $\{(1/n,1)\}$ shows it is not compact.

incorrect.message: $[0,1]$ is closed and bounded, hence compact. We want bounded but **not** compact, i.e. bounded and not closed.
$\mathbb{R}$ is unbounded, so it fails the *bounded* requirement entirely. Look for a bounded set that is not closed.
Compact = closed and bounded. 'Bounded but not compact' means bounded and **not closed** — an open interval works.

choices.label: Yes — it is closed (it contains its only limit point $0$) and bounded.
No — it has infinitely many points.
No — the points $1/n$ form an open cover with no finite subcover.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Its sole limit point is $0$, which is included, so the set is closed; it sits inside $[0,1]$, so it is bounded. Heine–Borel makes it compact.

incorrect.message: Infinitely many points is no obstacle: $[0,1]$ is infinite and compact. Apply Heine–Borel — check closed and bounded.
Single points are not open sets, so the $1/n$ do not form an open cover. The right test is closed and bounded, both of which hold here.
Find the limit points: the only one is $0$, and it is in the set, so the set is closed; it is also bounded. Hence compact.
